began on April 16, 1951. The regular season ended on October 3, with the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants_(baseball) "New York Giants (baseball)") and [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") as the regular season champions of the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") and [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League"), respectively. The Giants defeated the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers") in [a regular season best-of-three tiebreaker](/wiki/1951_National_League_tie-breaker_series "1951 National League tie-breaker series"), for the National League title, after both teams finished their 154-game schedules with identical 96–58 records. This was the third regular season tie-breaker, and saw a reversion from the single-game tie-breaker featured in [1948](/wiki/1948_American_League_tie-breaker_game "1948 American League tie-breaker game") to the three-game format featured in the [1946 tie-breaker series](/wiki/1946_National_League_tie-breaker_series "1946 National League tie-breaker series"). After splitting the first two games, the stage was set for a decisive third game, won in dramatic fashion on a [walk-off home run](/wiki/Walk-off_home_run "Walk-off home run") from the bat of Giant [Bobby Thomson](/wiki/Bobby_Thomson "Bobby Thomson"), one of the most famous moments in the history of baseball, commemorated as the "[Shot Heard 'Round the World](/wiki/Shot_Heard_%27Round_the_World_(baseball) "Shot Heard 'Round the World (baseball)")" and "The Miracle at [Coogan's Bluff](/wiki/Coogan%27s_Bluff "Coogan's Bluff")". The postseason began with Game 1 of the [48th World Series](/wiki/1951_World_Series "1951 World Series") on October 4 and ended with Game 6 on October 10. In the sixth iteration of this [Subway Series](/wiki/Subway_Series "Subway Series") World Series matchup, the Yankees defeated the Giants, four games to two, capturing their 14th championship in franchise history, and their third in a five-run World Series. This would be the final Subway Series matchup between the two teams, as the next World Series between the two in [1962](/wiki/1962_World_Series "1962 World Series") would see a [relocated Giants franchise](/wiki/San_Francisco_Giants "San Francisco Giants") in [San Francisco, California](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco").

The **1951 major league baseball season** began on April 16, 1951. The regular season ended on October 3, with the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants_(baseball) "New York Giants (baseball)") and [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") as the regular season champions of the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") and [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League"), respectively. The Giants defeated the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers") in [a regular season best-of-three tiebreaker](/wiki/1951_National_League_tie-breaker_series "1951 National League tie-breaker series"), for the National League title, after both teams finished their 154-game schedules with identical 96–58 records. This was the third regular season tie-breaker, and saw a reversion from the single-game tie-breaker featured in [1948](/wiki/1948_American_League_tie-breaker_game "1948 American League tie-breaker game") to the three-game format featured in the [1946 tie-breaker series](/wiki/1946_National_League_tie-breaker_series "1946 National League tie-breaker series"). After splitting the first two games, the stage was set for a decisive third game, won in dramatic fashion on a [walk-off home run](/wiki/Walk-off_home_run "Walk-off home run") from the bat of Giant [Bobby Thomson](/wiki/Bobby_Thomson "Bobby Thomson"), one of the most famous moments in the history of baseball, commemorated as the "[Shot Heard 'Round the World](/wiki/Shot_Heard_%27Round_the_World_(baseball) "Shot Heard 'Round the World (baseball)")" and "The Miracle at [Coogan's Bluff](/wiki/Coogan%27s_Bluff "Coogan's Bluff")". The postseason began with Game 1 of the [48th World Series](/wiki/1951_World_Series "1951 World Series") on October 4 and ended with Game 6 on October 10. In the sixth iteration of this [Subway Series](/wiki/Subway_Series "Subway Series") World Series matchup, the Yankees defeated the Giants, four games to two, capturing their 14th championship in franchise history, and their third in a five-run World Series. This would be the final Subway Series matchup between the two teams, as the next World Series between the two in [1962](/wiki/1962_World_Series "1962 World Series") would see a [relocated Giants franchise](/wiki/San_Francisco_Giants "San Francisco Giants") in [San Francisco, California](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco").

The [18th Major League Baseball All-Star Game](/wiki/1951_Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "1951 Major League Baseball All-Star Game") was held on July 10 at [Briggs Stadium](/wiki/Tiger_Stadium_(Detroit) "Tiger Stadium (Detroit)") in [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), home of the [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers"). The [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") won, 8–3.

On May 1, the [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox") become the sixth team in professional baseball to [break the color line](/wiki/Baseball_color_line "Baseball color line") when they fielded future [Hall-of-Famer](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame_and_Museum "National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum") [Minnie Miñoso](/wiki/Minnie_Mi%C3%B1oso "Minnie Miñoso").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

This was [Happy Chandler's](/wiki/Happy_Chandler "Happy Chandler") last season of his 5½-tenure as [Commissioner of Baseball](/wiki/Commissioner_of_Baseball "Commissioner of Baseball"), as he resigned on July 15, after team owner voted against renewing his contract which expired in April 1952. National League president [Ford Frick](/wiki/Ford_Frick "Ford Frick") would be elected as commissioner, officially taking the position September 20, just days before the end of the regular season.

## Schedule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1951_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Schedule")]

See also: [Major League Baseball schedule](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_schedule "Major League Baseball schedule")

The 1951 schedule consisted of 154 games for all teams in the American League and National League, each of which had eight teams. Each team was scheduled to play 22 games against the other seven teams of their respective league. This continued the format put in place since the [1904](/wiki/1904_Major_League_Baseball_season "1904 Major League Baseball season") season (except for [1919](/wiki/1919_Major_League_Baseball_season "1919 Major League Baseball season")) and would be used until [1961](/wiki/1961_Major_League_Baseball_season "1961 Major League Baseball season") in the American League and [1962](/wiki/1962_Major_League_Baseball_season "1962 Major League Baseball season") in the National League.

National League Opening Day took place on April 16, with a game between the [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates") and [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds "Cincinnati Reds"), while American League Opening Day took place the following day, featuring all eight teams. This was the first season since [1943](/wiki/1943_Major_League_Baseball_season "1943 Major League Baseball season") that both leagues opened on different days. The final day of the scheduled regular season was on September 30, which saw all sixteen teams play, continuing the trend from [1946](/wiki/1946_Major_League_Baseball_season "1946 Major League Baseball season"). Due to the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers") and [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants_(baseball) "New York Giants (baseball)") finishing with the same record of 96–58, a [best-of-three tie-breaker](/wiki/1951_National_League_tie-breaker_series "1951 National League tie-breaker series") was scheduled, to be considered an extension of the regular season, and took place between October 1 and October 3. The [World Series](/wiki/1951_World_Series "1951 World Series") took place between October 4 and October 10.

## Rule changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1951_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Rule changes")]

The 1951 season saw the following rule changes:

* The [bonus rule](/wiki/Bonus_rule "Bonus rule"), which stated that any player signed to a contract greater than $4,000 (equivalent to $49,600 in 2025) had to stay on the team's 25-man roster for two years, was abolished.[[2]](#cite_note-1950_Winter_SABR-2)
* In the event of a [walk](/wiki/Base_on_balls "Base on balls"), [hit by pitch](/wiki/Hit_by_pitch "Hit by pitch"), or other plays with the bases loaded which forces the runners to advance bases which wins the game for the team batting in the bottom of the final inning, the runner on third must touch [home base](/wiki/Baseball_field#Home_base "Baseball field") and the hitter of the current at bat must reach first base.[[3]](#cite_note-Retrosheet_Rules-3)
* Following a dead ball, play shall resume only when the [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher"), with the current ball, takes his place on the pitcher's mound.[[3]](#cite_note-Retrosheet_Rules-3)
* If a pitcher must come into a game to relieve the previous pitcher with no opportunity to warm up, he is allowed to throw as many warmup pitches on the field until the umpire feels the pitcher is properly prepared to face batters.[[3]](#cite_note-Retrosheet_Rules-3)
* If interference occurs by the catcher or fielder, and if so a [batter](/wiki/Batting_(baseball) "Batting (baseball)") or baserunner is awarded their next base, the catcher or fielder is charged with an [error](/wiki/Fielding_error "Fielding error").[[3]](#cite_note-Retrosheet_Rules-3)
* [Caught stealing](/wiki/Caught_stealing "Caught stealing") became a trackable statistic. Official rules surrounding caught stealing would not be codified until [1963](/wiki/1963_Major_League_Baseball_season "1963 Major League Baseball season").[[3]](#cite_note-Retrosheet_Rules-3)
